request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"vine::social_communication","name":"vine","one_liner":"Short-form 6-second looping video platform acquired by Twitter, shut down in 2017 after losing creators and ad revenue to competitors.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":1.5,"rationale":"Vine was a free, ad-supported short-form video platform with zero transaction fees. The pitch is a B2C marketplace taking a 20% transaction fee on paid 1:1 video consults. These are fundamentally different monetization architectures — ad revenue vs. transaction-fee marketplace."},"market":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both operate in the consumer social/creator space and both are concerned with influencer-to-fan relationships. Vine did pioneer the influencer economy and creator-audience dynamics. However, Vine was a global, mass-market UGC platform while the pitch targets a narrow geographic niche (Tokyo and Seoul) in the beauty vertical with a high-intent commerce use case."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Vine grew virally through open social sharing, embedding, and Twitter integration — classic B2C virality with no paywall. The pitch relies on beauty influencers acquiring fans and then converting them to paying consult clients, which is a supply-side recruitment + demand-side conversion GTM entirely unlike Vine's organic content virality."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both are early-stage consumer products targeting a specific creator/fan dynamic. Vine at launch was also a small team with rapid early growth. The pitch appears pre-scale, similar to Vine pre-launch. Beyond that, Vine's trajectory was mass-scale global whereas the pitch is deliberately niche and regional."}},"why_similar":"Both Vine and the pitch live at the intersection of content creators (influencers) and their audiences (fans), and both depend on retaining a supply side of creators to deliver value to consumers. Vine's failure to retain top creators — who fled to YouTube and Instagram — is directly analogous to the risk the pitch faces if beauty influencers migrate to competing platforms (e.g., TikTok LIVE, Instagram paid badges, or dedicated consult tools). The creator-retention problem is the core structural risk both share.","where_diverged":"The pitch diverges from Vine on nearly every material dimension: (1) Monetization — 20% transaction fee on paid consults vs. Vine's free/ad-revenue model; (2) Geography — deliberately Japan/Korea-focused (Tokyo, Seoul) vs. Vine's global footprint; (3) Use case — synchronous paid 1:1 video consultations vs. asynchronous public short-form video broadcast; (4) Customer type — Vine served mass consumers passively watching content, while the pitch targets fans willing to pay for personalized access; (5) Sector — the pitch is squarely beauty/commerce, while Vine was a general entertainment platform.","failure_causes":["creator exodus to better-monetized competing platforms (YouTube, Instagram)","absence of direct creator monetization tools","Twitter parent's strategic neglect and cost-cutting","advertiser/marketer flight to longer-form video platforms","inability to differentiate from Instagram Video and Snapchat","no direct revenue model for top creators, eliminating retention incentive"],"lessons_for_input":["Build creator-side monetization incentives first — the moment influencers can earn more elsewhere, they will leave, as Vine proved fatally; lock in your supply side with economics, not just audience size.","Do not rely on a parent platform's distribution or goodwill — Vine was killed by Twitter's internal priorities; keep your platform's destiny independent.","Establish defensible niche density (Tokyo + Seoul beauty) before expanding, rather than scaling broadly and losing differentiation as Vine did competing with Instagram and Snapchat on generic short video.","Paid 1:1 consults create a high-CAC conversion challenge — validate willingness-to-pay among fans before committing to transaction-fee infrastructure.","Monitor competing platforms (TikTok LIVE, Instagram paid DMs) obsessively; Vine's competitors copied its core feature and added monetization, eroding its reason to exist."]}
